channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Look up tonight: Jupiter and Venus will almost touch in one of 2026's best skywatching events - Live Science
item.description:
1. Look up tonight: Jupiter and Venus will almost touch in one of 2026's best skywatching events  Live Science
  2. Look up: 'Kissing planets' set to light up the night sky this week  FOX Weather
  3. Venus, Jupiter Converge in Stunning June 9th Dusk Conjunction  Sky & Telescope
  4. Don't miss Jupiter and Venus meet in a dazzling conjunction on June 9: Here's where and when to look  Space
  5. How to see the 'cosmic kiss' of Jupiter and Venus in the night sky  National Geographic

item.title: Bumblebees have tiny brains but they can solve problems like chimps and elephants - NPR
item.description:
1. Bumblebees have tiny brains but they can solve problems like chimps and elephants  NPR
  2. Bumblebees use tools to solve complex problems—despite not being trained to do so  Scientific American
  3. ‘They surprise me every time’: bees can use tools to solve problems, study finds  The Guardian
  4. Moment scientists taught bumblebees how to "play football"  Yahoo
  5. Bumblebees can solve problems on their own  Science News

  4. NASA to reveal Artemis III crew for next moon mission  WKMG

item.title: Atlantic 'cold blob' caused by weakening ocean current system that's likely nearing a tipping point, reanalysis finds - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Atlantic 'cold blob' caused by weakening ocean current system that's likely nearing a tipping point, reanalysis finds  Phys.org
  2. Mysterious ‘cold blob’ in the Atlantic suggests the AMOC is weakening  New Scientist
  3. Mysterious cold blob will 'disrupt life as we know it' across Europe  Metro.co.uk
  4. Atlantic “Cold Blob” highlights role of ocean circulation in climate change  Tech Explorist
  5. AMOC Alarm: An Unusual "Cold Blob" In North Atlantic Suggests Major Ocean Current System Is Dwindling  IFLScience

item.title: NASA interested in Hubble reboost if costs can be reduced - SpaceNews
item.description:
1. NASA interested in Hubble reboost if costs can be reduced  SpaceNews
  2. Robotic Spacecraft for Swift Boost Mission Arrives at NASA Wallops  NASA Science (.gov)
  3. Space tug will rescue the Swift and Hubble telescopes  Universe Space Tech
  4. NASA Eyes Hubble Orbit Boost to Keep the Legendary Telescope Alive Into the 2040s  Orbital Today
  5. NASA mission to rescue 21-year-old Swift space telescope on track as LINK arrives at launch site  starlust.org

item.title: The first humans on Mars will not just be explorers crossing a red desert. They will be radiation workers, dust-control technicians and weather-watchers on a planet where the danger is less dramatic than constant. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The first humans on Mars will not just be explorers crossing a red desert. They will be radiation workers, dust-control technicians and weather-watchers on a planet where the danger is less dramatic than constant.  Space Daily
  2. Are we ready to send humans to Mars?  The Planetary Society

item.title: 3I/ATLAS is only the third confirmed interstellar object ever detected passing through our solar system. It may be older than the Sun itself, and Webb observations show it is carrying an unusually carbon dioxide-rich coma as it swings past once before leaving fo - Space Daily
item.description:
1. 3I/ATLAS is only the third confirmed interstellar object ever detected passing through our solar system. It may be older than the Sun itself, and Webb observations show it is carrying an unusually carbon dioxide-rich coma as it swings past once before leaving fo  Space Daily
  2. SETI Institute Looks for Signs of Technology in Interstellar Visitor 3I/ATLAS  SETI
  3. NASA’s Webb Detects Methane on Interstellar Comet 3I/ATLAS  NASA Science (.gov)
  4. Radio scans find no alien tech from the latest interstellar comet  AP News
  5. Scientists probe cosmic visitor from deep space, come up empty in search for alien life  Los Angeles Times

item.title: Early humans used fire in Wonderwerk Cave up to 1.8 million years ago - Archaeology News Online Magazine
item.description:
1. Early humans used fire in Wonderwerk Cave up to 1.8 million years ago  Archaeology News Online Magazine
  2. Humans may have discovered fire hundreds of thousands of years before previously thought: new research  New York Post
  3. New study finds hominids used fire 700,000 years earlier than previously recorded  Interesting Engineering
  4. Hominids used fire 700,000 years earlier than previously thought  Science Media Centre España
  5. Homo erectus campfires in South Africa reconstructed in 3D  Foro3D

item.title: Researchers identify world’s largest scorpion that roamed Earth 415 million years ago - Interesting Engineering
item.description:

item.title: Trouble near the Milky Way: The Large Magellanic Cloud is ripping its smaller neighbor galaxy apart - Space
item.description:

  4. New Stellar Motion Map Proves the Small Magellanic Cloud is Expanding  AZoQuantum

item.title: Mysterious signals keep coming from space: Astronomers find their 'Rosetta stone' - Phys.org
item.description:

  4. Scientists decode strange, repeating radio signal from space  Live Science
  5. Scientists have finally deciphered a mysterious radio signal from deep space  NEWS.am TECH

item.title: There are millions of objects in our own solar system that have never been seen and the facility that will find them is now just months from starting its decade-long survey - Space Daily
item.description: There are millions of objects in our own solar system that have never been seen and the facility that will find them is now just months from starting its decade-long survey  Space Daily

item.title: Some Australian Aboriginal stories may remember coastlines that vanished beneath the sea over 10,000 years ago. Researchers think they could be among humanity’s oldest surviving true stories. - Space Daily
item.description: Some Australian Aboriginal stories may remember coastlines that vanished beneath the sea over 10,000 years ago. Researchers think they could be among humanity’s oldest surviving true stories.  Space Daily

item.description: A 'naked singularity' problem that vexed Stephen Hawking takes a step closer to reality  Live Science

item.title: Mount Etna eruptions reveal carbon dioxide and water can trigger separate explosive paths - Phys.org
item.description: Mount Etna eruptions reveal carbon dioxide and water can trigger separate explosive paths  Phys.org

item.title: In 1959, the Soviet Luna 3 probe swung around the far side of the Moon and took 29 grainy photographs of a hemisphere humans had never seen, then developed the film onboard and scanned the negatives with a flying-spot beam to radio them back across - Space Daily
item.description: In 1959, the Soviet Luna 3 probe swung around the far side of the Moon and took 29 grainy photographs of a hemisphere humans had never seen, then developed the film onboard and scanned the negatives with a flying-spot beam to radio them back across  Space Daily

item.title: The International Space Station circles the planet so fast that the crew watch the sun rise and set sixteen times in a single day, a new dawn roughly every ninety minutes - Space Daily
item.description: The International Space Station circles the planet so fast that the crew watch the sun rise and set sixteen times in a single day, a new dawn roughly every ninety minutes  Space Daily

item.title: Why the years seem to speed up as we age, and how focusing on one thing might make time feel slower again - Space Daily
item.description: Why the years seem to speed up as we age, and how focusing on one thing might make time feel slower again  Space DailySee more headlines & perspectives on Google News

item.title: Chemists have demonstrated for the first time how RNA may have copied itself on early Earth — solving a bottleneck that had blocked the origin-of-life field for decades - Space Daily
item.description: Chemists have demonstrated for the first time how RNA may have copied itself on early Earth — solving a bottleneck that had blocked the origin-of-life field for decades  Space Daily

item.title: The largest insects that ever lived were dragonflies with wingspans of more than two feet, grown in an ancient atmosphere so much richer in oxygen that nothing that size could survive in the air we breathe today - Space Daily
item.description: The largest insects that ever lived were dragonflies with wingspans of more than two feet, grown in an ancient atmosphere so much richer in oxygen that nothing that size could survive in the air we breathe today  Space Daily

item.title: Predictive surrogates could cut quantum computing measurement overhead by more than 99.97% - Phys.org
item.description: Predictive surrogates could cut quantum computing measurement overhead by more than 99.97%  Phys.org

item.title: Scientists Simulated Nuclear Fallout in The Lab And Got a Surprise - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Scientists Simulated Nuclear Fallout in The Lab And Got a Surprise  ScienceAlert
  2. What happens seconds after a nuclear explosion continues to surprise experts: the key lies in radioactive fallout  Revista Merca2.0
  3. Scientists simulated a nuclear fireball and found a surprise in the fallout  ScienceDaily
  4. Scientists simulate nuclear fireball in the laboratory and discover a surprise in the radioactive fallout by observing how cesium, uranium, and cerium change when they remain at high temperatures for a longer time.  CPG Click Petróleo e Gás
  5. Science breakthrough: Scientists create a miniature nuclear fireball – and make a surprise discovery  GB News

item.title: The James Webb telescope keeps finding early galaxies that look brighter, bigger and more mature than astronomers expected, forcing researchers to rethink how quickly the first galaxies formed stars and assembled after the Big Bang. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The James Webb telescope keeps finding early galaxies that look brighter, bigger and more mature than astronomers expected, forcing researchers to rethink how quickly the first galaxies formed stars and assembled after the Big Bang.  Space Daily
  2. Why Webb Is Rewriting The Story Of The Early Universe  WorldAtlas
  3. The Galaxy That Forgot to Spin  Universe Today
  4. 'Astonishing': James Webb telescope spots the most chemically primitive galaxy in the ancient universe  Live Science

item.title: From the smallest to the biggest objects in space - Big Think
item.description: From the smallest to the biggest objects in space  Big Think
